stories.headline: Denver Mayor Johnston backs moratorium on new data center construction pending regulatory review
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston announced Monday that the city is considering a temporary halt on new data center projects, pending a review of regulations governing the facilities. The pause, intended to last several months, would allow city officials to develop new data center-specific zoning and rules focused on responsible land, energy and water use, as well as utility rate affordability for residents.

The moratorium would take effect only if a majority of the 13-member city council approves an enabling ordinance. Council members Paul Kashmann and Darrell Watson are quoted in the mayor's release as generally supportive, though Kashmann noted there remains "distance between the mayor's view and mine" on the particulars.

The proposal would not affect existing data centers, those already under construction or projects already permitted by the city. A controversial CoreSite data center under construction in the Elyria-Swansea neighborhood of north Denver would not be immediately affected, according to city spokesman Jon Ewing.

City officials plan to consult with residents, climate experts and industry leaders in crafting new regulations. Johnston's move comes as Colorado state lawmakers are separately wrestling with data center policy, reflecting growing concern statewide over the facilities' significant electricity and water demands.

stories.headline: North Denver residents rally against CoreSite data center, demand accountability over power and water use
stories.summary:
Dozens of residents from Denver's Globeville, Elyria and Swansea neighborhoods gathered recently to protest a multistory CoreSite data center under construction at East 49th Avenue and Race Street. Neighbors voiced concerns about higher electricity bills, increased blackouts, emissions from emergency generators and the facility's water consumption, and presented the company with a formal list of terms they want CoreSite to abide by as conditions of being a "good neighbor."

Nearly all homes in the three neighborhoods sit within 1.5 miles of the future campus, which will offer more than 590,000 square feet of server space when completed. At full buildout, projected for the 2030s pending customer demand, the three-building facility could draw up to 75 megawatts of power — equivalent to roughly 82,500 homes — and up to 805,000 gallons of water per day for cooling.

Coresite, which is headquartered in Denver, says it is extremely rare for its centers to operate at maximum capacity, with typical usage below 50 percent. Construction on the first floor of the initial building is expected to finish in June, and the company says the center will not be used for artificial intelligence model training.

The rally coincided with Mayor Johnston's announcement of a proposed citywide moratorium on new data center construction, though Johnston's office confirmed the CoreSite project would not be immediately affected because it is already under construction.

stories.headline: Colorado ballot initiative would enshrine public records and open meetings rights in state constitution
stories.summary:
A statewide citizen ballot initiative formally filed last week would embed in the Colorado Constitution a new section declaring that "the fundamental right of the public to know the affairs of all levels of state and local government shall apply to all public records and public meetings." The measure was filed by leaders of the Independence Institute and the League of Women Voters of Colorado and is backed by an unusual coalition that also includes the ACLU of Colorado, Colorado Common Cause, the Colorado Broadcasters Association and the Colorado Press Association.

If approved by voters in the fall, the measure would render inapplicable any local ordinance, rule, regulation, charter or statute that conflicts with the new constitutional provision. Proponents say that would effectively nullify a controversial 2024 law passed by the legislature that shielded some of its deliberations from public view.

The initiative would impose civil penalties of at least $1,000 per offense on any public official, officer, employee or agent of government who knowingly violates its provisions. At least nine other states have enacted similar constitutional transparency clauses, according to the supporting alliance.

The measure must first undergo a review and comment hearing with staff of the Colorado General Assembly's Legislative Council before proceeding to the state Title Board for certification.

stories.headline: Historic Loretto Heights administration building eyed for $70M rent-to-own housing conversion
stories.summary:
Developer Commún plans to convert the historic Administration Building at the former Loretto Heights campus in southwest Denver into 50 rent-to-own apartments, part of a broader redevelopment effort at the site. The project, valued at approximately $70 million, would represent a rare application of the rent-to-own model in Denver's housing market.

The conversion is part of ongoing efforts to adaptively reuse the historic Loretto Heights campus, which has been the subject of multiple redevelopment proposals in recent years. Separately, the Luzzatto Company has detailed plans to convert two historic downtown Denver towers into a mixed-use development featuring more than 700 apartments and community amenities.

The rent-to-own model is intended to provide a pathway to homeownership for residents who may not qualify for traditional mortgages, addressing one of Denver's persistent affordability challenges. Denver's average home price has recently been reported at more than six times the median family income.

Additional details on financing, timeline and eligibility for prospective residents were not disclosed in initial reporting.

stories.headline: Denver Summit FC unveils inaugural 'Evergreen Kit' at Union Station ahead of NWSL debut
stories.summary:
Denver Summit FC on Monday revealed its first official jersey, the "Evergreen Kit," at a public event at Union Station attended by fans, players and VIP guests. The primary kit features a rich evergreen green inspired by Colorado's pine forests, with light blue and sage accents drawn from the state's sky and high desert plains. Center back Kaleigh Kurtz and forward Ally Brazier modeled the kit, and a performance by Colorado Symphony musicians accompanied the unveiling.

Controlling owner Rob Cohen noted that the entire franchise — from the NWSL expansion award to Monday's kit launch — was assembled within 12 months. The club still has not announced a front-of-jersey sponsor, a key revenue source for professional soccer clubs, though Cohen said an announcement is forthcoming.

Denver Summit FC's first home match is scheduled for Saturday, March 28, at Empower Field at Mile High against the Washington Spirit. The club has already sold more than 45,000 tickets and opened the upper bowl for additional sales, putting the team on track to break the NWSL single-game attendance record of 40,091 set by Bay FC last year.

"I don't want to just break the record," Cohen said. "I want to set a record that will stand forever." The team's only stated concern is weather, given Denver's historically unpredictable late-March conditions.

stories.headline: Avalanche players return from Winter Olympics reflecting on gold-medal final, Landeskog's comeback
stories.summary:
Colorado Avalanche players returned to practice Sunday after the 2026 Winter Olympics in Milan, where eight members of the club competed for five countries. The United States defeated Canada in overtime for the gold medal in what players described as an exceptional tournament, with four of seven elimination games going to extra time.

A notable storyline for Avalanche fans was captain Gabe Landeskog's return to action with Sweden. Landeskog had missed three full regular seasons with knee issues before sustaining an upper-body injury Jan. 4; he played five Olympic games, recording two goals and four points. Sweden was eliminated in the quarterfinals by a Quinn Hughes overtime goal for the Americans.

Martin Necas was Czechia's standout performer with three goals and a team-high eight points in five games before Canada's Mitch Marner ended their run in overtime in the quarterfinals. Brock Nelson won gold for the United States, while Nathan MacKinnon, Cale Makar and Devon Toews took silver with Canada. Artturi Lehkonen and Joel Kiviranta claimed bronze with Finland.

"It was unreal," Necas said after returning to practice. "It was something I, and all of us that were there, had always dreamt of." The Avalanche, the NHL's top team at the Olympic break, now return their focus to the regular season ahead of the March 7 trade deadline.

stories.headline: Nuggets face pivotal offseason decision on restricted free agent Peyton Watson
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets face a significant offseason roster question surrounding fourth-year forward Peyton Watson, whose breakout stretch of play has made him one of the team's most valuable contributors but also a player likely to command a contract the club may struggle to fit under its salary cap structure. The Nuggets hold Watson's full Bird rights and the right to match any offer sheet he receives as a restricted free agent, meaning they are not obligated to let him walk.

However, retaining Watson without trading another significant contract would likely require the Nuggets to exceed the NBA's second apron luxury tax threshold, a line the franchise has consistently declined to cross under the current collective bargaining agreement. Watson himself cited the second apron as a reason he was not extended before the season began.

Denver Post beat writer Bennett Durando, responding to reader questions, identified Cam Johnson as the most likely trade candidate to create cap relief, though he noted Johnson would return the least value in a deal. Whether Christian Braun could also be on the table is a question the organization has not publicly addressed.

The Nuggets' dilemma is compounded by injuries to veteran Aaron Gordon, which have underscored the need for a reliable third offensive option behind Nikola Jokic and Jamal Murray heading into next season.

stories.headline: Near-record warmth, winds up to 80 mph and elevated fire danger forecast across Colorado this week
stories.summary:
The National Weather Service has issued a High Wind Watch for the Front Range mountains and northern Front Range foothills effective Tuesday morning through late afternoon, with sustained winds of 35 to 50 mph and gusts up to 80 mph possible in parts of Jackson, Larimer, Grand, Boulder, Gilpin, Clear Creek, Summit and Park counties. Forecasters warned the winds could down trees and power lines, cause outages and make travel hazardous, particularly for high-profile vehicles.

Denver is expected to approach or tie record high temperatures for late February, with a forecast high of 70 degrees on Tuesday — one degree below the Feb. 23 record set in 2002 — and 67 degrees on Wednesday, close to the Feb. 24 record of 71 degrees set in 1995.

The warm, dry and windy conditions are raising fire weather concerns across Colorado's Eastern Plains. A fire weather watch was planned for Tuesday in southeastern Colorado, including Pueblo, Walsenburg and Trinidad, where wind gusts up to 30 mph and humidity values as low as 10 percent are forecast. Forecasters urged extreme caution with any outdoor burning, warning that "fires will catch and spread quickly."

The active weather pattern is expected to persist through the end of the work week, with the mountains and northern plains potentially seeing additional periods of strong wind and occasional snow showers, while the Denver metro area remains mostly dry but breezy.

stories.headline: Denver and Colorado lower flags to honor Rev. Jesse Jackson, who died at 84
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston ordered flags to fly at half-staff through Saturday in honor of the Rev. Jesse Jackson, the civil rights leader and two-time presidential candidate who died Feb. 18 at age 84 at his home in Chicago. Governor Jared Polis separately ordered flags on all state public buildings to be lowered from sunrise on March 6 to sunset on March 7, coinciding with Jackson's public and private memorial services in Chicago.

Johnston called Jackson "a titan of the Civil Rights Movement, a ferocious advocate, and a fearless trailblazer whose 'Rainbow Coalition' changed our nation forever." Polis noted Jackson's connections to Colorado civil rights leaders including Wellington and Wilma Webb, Cleo Parker Robinson and former Denver Mayor Michael Hancock.

Jackson led the Southern Christian Leadership Conference and founded the Rainbow PUSH Coalition, running for the Democratic presidential nomination in 1984 and 1988. He died surrounded by family, his daughter confirmed.

A public celebration of life ceremony is scheduled for March 6 at the 10,000-seat House of Hope church in Chicago, followed by a private memorial on March 7.

stories.headline: ICE arrests Grand Junction man with alleged MS-13 ties who is wanted in El Salvador
stories.summary:
U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents arrested Juan Carlos Membreno-Portillo in Grand Junction on Sunday, ICE's Denver field office confirmed. Officials described Membreno-Portillo as a known member of the Mara Salvatrucha criminal organization, also known as MS-13, and said he is wanted in El Salvador.

Membreno-Portillo will remain in ICE custody pending removal proceedings, the agency said in a post on X. No additional details about the circumstances of the arrest or specific charges were disclosed.

The arrest comes as immigration enforcement activity in Mesa County has been significant in recent years. According to the Deportation Data Project, at least 151 ICE arrests were recorded in Mesa County in 2025.

ICE's Denver area of responsibility covers Colorado and Wyoming, and the agency has been conducting enforcement operations across both states in recent months.

stories.headline: Colorado Medicaid fraud: $25 million in abnormal billing triggers criminal charges against transportation providers
stories.summary:
Colorado Medicaid officials detected $25 million in abnormal billing over a four-month period two years ago, a surge that prompted a new state law and the suspension of dozens of providers in what authorities now characterize as an organized fraud ring. State and federal prosecutors have since brought criminal charges against two transportation providers in separate indictments.

The fraudulent billing scheme prompted the legislature to pass new oversight measures aimed at tightening accountability within Colorado's Medicaid transportation reimbursement system. Dozens of providers were suspended as the investigation expanded.

Details of the two indictments, including the identities of the charged providers and specific allegations, were not fully disclosed in initial reporting. The cases represent some of the most significant Medicaid fraud enforcement actions in the state in recent years.

Colorado's Medicaid program covers hundreds of thousands of low-income residents, and transportation benefits are intended to help enrollees reach medical appointments. Officials have not disclosed whether additional charges are anticipated as the investigation continues.

stories.headline: Fort Carson soldier's 2006 death in sewage pond remains unsolved; widow says it was no accident
stories.summary:
Amber Stone reported her husband, Army Sgt. Joseph Eric Barker, missing on Valentine's Day 2006. A week later, he was found dead in a sewage treatment pond on Fort Carson. Stone and Barker's mother, Lynda, have long maintained that Barker could not have walked barefoot across the installation in winter and entered the pond of his own accord.

The case has remained unresolved for two decades, with the family disputing any conclusion that Barker's death was accidental or self-inflicted. No public ruling of homicide has been made, and no charges have been brought in connection with his death.

The story surfaces two decades later amid renewed attention to cold cases and unexplained deaths on military installations across the United States. Fort Carson is located in Colorado Springs, south of Denver.

Neither the Army nor federal investigators have publicly commented on any new developments in the Barker case. The family continues to seek answers about the circumstances of his death.

comic.teaser: "“Well, according to the dictionary, I’m just a large, flightless bird from Ea..." + 3 more
